---
title: "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity"
shorttitle: "Bayesian models of thermal tolerance"
authors:
  - name: "Daniel W.A. Noble"
    email: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Pieter A. Arnold"
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Patrice Pottier"
    email: patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se
    affiliations:
      - ref: gu
      - ref: unsw
affiliations:
  - id: anu
    name: Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University
    city: Canberra
    country: Australia
  - id: gu
    name: Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg
    city: Gothenburg
    country: Sweden
  - id: unsw
    name: Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales
    city: Sydney
    state: New South Wales
    country: Australia
abstract: |
  Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models link heat intensity and exposure duration to organismal tolerance, but are usually fit with a two-stage analysis that often discards uncertainty, mishandles censored or overdispersed data, and limits hierarchical inference and prediction. We derive analytical equivalences showing how four-parameter logistic and related dose-response models recover classical TDT/TLS quantities, including $z$, $CT_{max}$, $T_{crit}$, heat injury and survival, as summaries of a single fitted surface. We implement this in `bayesTLS`, a Bayesian 4PL model for raw count or proportion data. Simulations show the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. Case studies in shrimp, snow gum, zebrafish and *Drosophila* estimate group contrasts and predict survival under field traces. Our new approach provides more robust inference, greater flexibility and more opportunities to better understand the consequences of extreme heat.
keywords:
  - thermal death time
  - dose-response
  - thermal tolerance landscape
  - hierarchical Bayesian model
  - 4-parameter logistic
  - heat injury
  - critical thermal maximum
  - ectotherm
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  docx:
    reference-doc: ../bib/templates/template.docx
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    fig-pos: "H"
    colorlinks: true
    link-citations: true
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % tinytable LaTeX dependencies. See supplement.qmd for rationale.
        \usepackage{tabularray}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayUnderline}[1]{\underline{#1}}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayStrikeout}[1]{\sout{#1}}
        % Author block: Quarto's default \author{names \and …} gives
        % names only. We override with authblk to pin affiliations from
        % YAML to the title page.
        \usepackage[affil-it]{authblk}
        \renewcommand\Authsep{, }
        \renewcommand\Authand{, }
        \renewcommand\Authands{, }
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\itshape\small}
        \let\OldAuthor\author
        \renewcommand{\author}[1]{}
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Daniel W.A. Noble\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{daniel.noble@anu.edu.au}}}%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Pieter A. Arnold}%
          \OldAuthor[2,3]{Patrice Pottier}%
          \affil[1]{Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia}%
          \affil[2]{Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden}%
          \affil[3]{Evolution \& Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia}%
        }
bibliography: ../bib/tdt_problems.bib
csl: ../bib/ecology-letters.csl
link-citations: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Daniel W.A. Noble**^1,\*^, **Pieter A. Arnold**^1^, **Patrice Pottier**^2,3^

^1^Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia.

^2^Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden.

^3^Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.

^\*^Corresponding author: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au

**Abstract**

Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models link heat intensity and exposure duration to organismal tolerance, but are usually fit with a two-stage analysis that often discards uncertainty, mishandles censored or overdispersed data, and limits hierarchical inference and prediction. We derive analytical equivalences showing how four-parameter logistic and related dose-response models recover classical TDT/TLS quantities, including $z$, $CT_{max}$, $T_{crit}$, heat injury and survival, as summaries of a single fitted surface. We implement this in `bayesTLS`, a Bayesian 4PL model for raw count or proportion data. Simulations show the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. Case studies in shrimp, snow gum, zebrafish and *Drosophila* estimate group contrasts and predict survival under field traces. Our new approach provides more robust inference, greater flexibility and more opportunities to better understand the consequences of extreme heat.

**Keywords:** thermal death time; dose-response; thermal tolerance landscape; hierarchical Bayesian model; 4-parameter logistic; heat injury; critical thermal maximum; ectotherm
:::

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| cache: false

# Project-wide objects loaded for inline reporting throughout the manuscript.
# Every number, table, and figure is produced from code — no magic numbers.

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)
library(brms)
library(posterior)
library(tinytable)

# Force tinytable to register its LaTeX preamble dependencies on every
# render. See supplement.qmd setup-supp for explanation.
invisible(tinytable::tt(data.frame(x = 1)))

set.seed(123)

# Shared figure theme: keep all manuscript figures visually consistent.
# Define once here; load via source() into every figure chunk.
# source(here::here("R", "theme_ms.R"))

# Per-group z / CTmax medians written by ms/supplement.qmd's Figure 4 build
# (output/data/fig4_summary.rds). Used for inline reporting so headline numbers
# are produced by code, not typed. Same render dependency as the Fig 4/5 PNGs:
# render the supplement first to (re)generate it.
fig4_summary <- readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "fig4_summary.rds"))

# CTmax_1hr across the 1-hour-reference case studies (shrimp, zebrafish stages,
# leaf PSII; the vinegar fly is reported at 4 h and excluded here).
ctmax1h    <- fig4_summary$median[fig4_summary$quantity == "CTmax_1hr"]
ctmax1h_lo <- round(min(ctmax1h), 1)
ctmax1h_hi <- round(max(ctmax1h), 1)
```

# Introduction

Climate change is increasing the frequency, intensity, and duration of extreme heat events globally, with cascading consequences for organismal fitness, population persistence, and biodiversity [@snook_fertility_2026; @arnold_thermal_2025; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @smale_marine_2019; @wiens_localextinctions_2016]. Quantifying variation in thermal tolerance within and among species is central to forecasting biological responses to climate change in nature [@sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019; @jorgensen_nature_2022]. Threshold-based measures of heat tolerance, such as the critical thermal maximum ($CT_{max}$), have long been used to establish differences in climate vulnerability among taxa and predicting vulnerability to climate change [@pottier_amphibians_2025; @sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019]. While useful, threshold-based metrics do not capture how the biological effects of extreme heat on fitness depend on both the duration and intensity of heat stress, limiting our ability to make realistic predictions of the impacts of climate change in nature [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021].

The Thermal Death Time (TDT) / Thermal Load Sensitivity (TLS) models address this limitation by quantifying how the intensity and duration of heat exposure jointly determine survival responses to heat stress [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @arnold_thermal_2025]. Recently, the TDT framework was expanded to include sublethal (e.g., fertility, heat coma) responses to heat stress, providing a flexible framework to broadly explain variation in biological responses to heat stress [@orsted_suzukii_2024; @arnold_thermal_2025]. These models capture how heat injury accumulates in a dose-dependent manner above a certain threshold ($T_{crit}$), with injury accumulating more rapidly at higher temperatures and over longer exposures. As a result, organisms may eventually reach a thermal load that leads to compromised physiological function that negatively impacts growth, reproduction and survival [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_landscapes_2014; @arnold_thermal_2025; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019] if injury cannot be repaired [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @jorgensen_unifying_2021]. By linking biological responses to the intensity and duration of heat stress, the TLS and TDT frameworks allow for more realistic predictions of the impacts of  fluctuating thermal regimes [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021], and have shown promise in predicting observed mortality rates in nature [@rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @zamora2026].

Despite their predictive power, TDT/TLS analyses traditionally involves a two-step modelling approach [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023]. First, organisms are exposed to multiple temperatures for different durations, and the proportion of individuals that are dead, infertile, knocked down, or otherwise affected is modelled as a function of exposure duration. This is typically done by fitting independent dose-response curves (usually models with strong assumptions) at each assay temperature ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]A). From these curves, the exposure duration required to reach a specified response threshold is estimated, most often $t_{50}$, the time required to reach 50% mortality, infertility, knockdown, or some other endpoint. Second, the resulting $\log_{10}(t_{50})$ estimates are regressed against assay temperature. Thermal sensitivity (i.e., the inverse of the slope of this regression, $z$) and static critical thermal maximum at time $t$ (i.e., the intercept of the regression, $sCT_{max_{t}}$) are then derived from this linear model [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022] ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]B). Thermal sensitivity ($z$) is interpreted as the temperature increase required to reduce $t_{50}$ by a factor of 10, while $sCT_{max_{t}}$ is usually arbitrarily computed for a 1 min [@rezende_landscapes_2014], 1 hour [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019], or 4 hour [@orsted_suzukii_2024] duration of heat stress exposure. These quantities can then be used to estimate cumulative Heat Injury (HI) under realistic temperature-time series, where injury accumulates as a function of the exposure duration relative to the predicted tolerance time at each temperature [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @villeneuve_dynamic_2024]. Recent extensions have emphasized that injury accumulation may also be partly offset by repair or acclimation mechanisms, motivating the emergence of new models that incorporate damage-repair dynamics [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @orsted2026].

The two-step pipeline is intuitive and easy to implement, yet it has several practical and statistical limitations [for a recent discussion of some issues see @bullard2026]. First, the two-step pipeline does not typically allow proper uncertainty propagation. The $t_{50}$ estimates derived in the first step have non-negligible sampling variance, but they are commonly treated as fixed point estimates in the second-step regression. Neglecting sampling variance can bias or miscalibrate inference on $z$ and $sCT_{max}$, and lead to inflated $R^2$ values. Second, estimating $t_{50}$ from dose-response models requires empirical data spanning a large mortality range (preferably ranging from 0 to 100% mortality) at every assayed temperature to anchor the upper and lower asymptotes of the dose-response curve. When data are sparse or do not span the full mortality range, $t_{50}$ estimates can be incorrectly estimated or undefined, which is then propagated to bias and miscalibrated estimates of uncertainty in $z$ and $CT_{max}$. Third, group comparisons (between species, sexes, or life stages) inherit this miscalibrated uncertainty. Reducing each dose-response curve to a fixed $t_{50}$ estimate makes it difficult to determine if differences in thermal tolerance and sensitivity between groups can be attributed to differences in the upper asymptote, lower asymptote, or curve steepness of the dose-response curve. Curve steepness, for example, has recently been shown to affect predictions [@bullard2026]. Fourth, fitting separate dose-response models for each assay temperature and subsequently regressing $t_{50}$ estimates against temperature assumes that these estimates are independent, and typically ignores the hierarchical structure of experimental data (e.g., individuals nested within vials, plates, tanks, clutches, batches, populations, or experimental days). Ignoring this nesting structure prevents the decomposition of variance, and can lead to pseudoreplication and anticonservative uncertainty estimates, particularly when replicate-level variation affects the shape or position of the dose-response curve [@bullard2026]. Finally, the two-step pipeline does not allow downstream predictions of heat injury and survival under fluctuating thermal regimes with uncertainty, which is key to inferring the confidence in our predictions of the impacts of extreme heat impacts. 

Here, we present a single-stage alternative to the classical two-step pipeline: a hierarchical Bayesian four-parameter logistic model fitted jointly to all temperature-by-duration response data. In this framework, survival or sublethal response probabilities are estimated directly as a function of exposure duration and assay temperature, while allowing the parameters of the dose-response curve to vary among groups and hierarchical levels. The four-parameter logistic model provides a flexible sigmoidal description of thermal dose responses, with interpretable parameters for the lower asymptote, upper asymptote, curve steepness, and midpoint [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_ecotox_2010; @nielsen_nlmm_2004; @rudemo_random_1989; @ritz_streibig_2008]. We focus on binomial and beta-binomial outcomes because count data dominate TDT/TLS studies, but the same modelling logic can be extended to other response types using appropriate likelihoods, including continuous performance traits, or latency data. Similarly, although we use the four-parameter log-logistic form for its flexibility, simpler nested models (3PL, with one asymptote fixed; 2PL, with both fixed at 0 and 1) or alternative monotonic dose-response functions (e.g., Weibull, log-normal) can be implemented within the same general framework [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_streibig_2008], however, we show that assumptions of these simpler models can affect TDT/TLS parameter estimations.

We show how classical TDT/TLS quantities, such as thermal sensitivity ($z$), thermal tolerance ($CT_{max}$), critical temperature thresholds ($T_{crit}$), heat injury accumulation, survival under fluctuating thermal regimes, and their uncertainty, can be derived from this single Bayesian model. We develop these tools into an R package $bayesTLS$, and recommend this new modelling approach as the default approach for analysing organismal responses to varying thermal loads. Using simulations and empirical case studies in brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*) photosystem II function, zebrafish (*Danio rerio*), and *Drosophila suzukii*, we demonstrate how to implement, validate, and expand this new modelling approach.

```{r}
#| label: fig-joint-4pl-conceptual
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Conceptual comparison of the classical two-stage TDT/TLS workflow and the joint Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model. (a) In the two-stage workflow, separate dose-response curves are fit at each assay temperature and the resulting $t_{50}$ estimates are regressed against temperature. This can fail when assays do not bracket $t_{50}$, treats first-stage estimates as fixed and independent, and does not naturally propagate uncertainty, covariates, or hierarchical structure into $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$. (b) The joint 4PL model estimates the full survival surface in one model. Coloured curves show posterior median survival probabilities at assay temperatures, with shaded pointwise 95% credible intervals. Filled circles mark the model midpoint, `mid(T)`, defining relative $\\log_{10}t_{50}$; open triangles mark the absolute 50% survival crossing. The black and steelblue trajectories give the relative and absolute $t_{50}$ relationships used to derive $z_{rel}$, $z_{abs}$, and $CT_{max,1h}$ at the 60-min reference. Dashed ghost curves show the fitted dose response at the corresponding relative and absolute $CT_{max,1h}$ values. Inset: the 4PL parameters `low`, `up`, `mid`, and `k`. Values are illustrative."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "65%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig1.png"))
```

# A non-linear Bayesian hierarchical model for characterizing thermal load sensitivity

## Model specification

Assuming we have mortality data for different times and temperatures, let $y$ denote the number of survivors out of $n$ individuals exposed for time $t$ (e.g., minutes) at assay temperature $T$ (°C). We can model the counts directly with a binomial likelihood (but a beta-binomial likelihood function can also be used and controls for overdispersion):

$$
y \mid n, p \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n,\, p),
$$ {#eq-likelihood}

with survival probability $p$ given by a four-parameter logistic (4PL) function of $\log_{10}$-exposure time,

$$
p \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10} t - \text{mid})\bigr)}.
$$ {#eq-4pl}

where $\text{low}$ is the lower survival probability asymptote, $\text{up}$ is the upper survival probability asymptote, and $k$ is the slope defining the change in survival probability on the $\log_{10} t$ axis. Larger $k$ gives a sharper decline in survival probability with $\log_{10} t$. $\text{mid}$ is the value of $\log_{10} t$ at which $p$ falls to the midpoint between $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$. While other logistic forms can be used (e.g., two or 3 parameter logistics), @eq-4pl is the most flexible model that can account for different asymptotic survival probabilities.

# Deriving key thermal load sensitivity parameters 

The TDT/TLS frameworks are elegant in that they derive metrics with clear biological interpretation that can be compared across populations and species (e.g., *z*, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$). Here, we show that we can derive these same metrics directly from @eq-4pl. 

As indicated above, we can model changes in the mean of any of the four parameters in @eq-4pl. Thermal sensitivity (*z*) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ can be derived by modelling the effect of temperature on midpoints specifically ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]C), as follows:

$$
\text{mid} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,(T - \bar{T}),
$$ {#eq-mid-temp}

where $\text{mid}$ is the $\log_{10}$-time at which survival equals the midpoint between the lower and upper asymptotes at assay temperature $T$ (°C); $\beta_0$ is the intercept giving the midpoint at the grand mean temperature $\bar{T}$; $\beta_1$ is the slope describing how the midpoint changes per °C increase in $T$; and $\bar{T}$ is the grand mean of assay temperatures used to mean-centre $T$, which decorrelates $\beta_0$ from $\beta_1$ and improves parameter estimation and interpretation [@schielzeth_centering_2010].

When the experimental design estimates upper and lower limits of the response (i.e., $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$) near 0 and 1, the 4PL midpoint is equivalent to the classical $t_{50}$: the time at which survival reaches 50%. In this case, @eq-mid-temp directly gives thermal sensitivity as $z = -1/\beta_1$, and the critical thermal limit at any reference time $t_\text{ref}$ is $CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) = \bar{T} + (\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0)/\beta_1$ (with $t_\text{ref} = 60$ min for $CT_{max_{1hr}}$).

More generally, the 4PL distinguishes between two related threshold definitions ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]C). A *relative* threshold is defined by each curve's own midpoint, $p = (\text{up}+\text{low})/2$. On this scale, $\text{mid}(T)$ is exactly the relative $\log_{10}t_{50}$, so $z_\text{rel} = -1/\beta_1$ and $CT_{max,\text{rel}}$ is read directly from @eq-mid-temp. An *absolute* threshold instead asks when survival reaches a fixed probability, such as 50% survival, regardless of the fitted asymptotes. When $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$ differ from 0 and 1, this absolute threshold no longer coincides with the midpoint and requires an asymmetry correction (see [Supplement Section 10](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#derivation-of-the-correction-factors-for-threshold-summaries)):

$$
\log_{10} t_{50}(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-t50}

The corresponding absolute $CT_{max}$ is obtained by applying the same correction on the temperature scale:

$$
CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) \;=\; \bar{T} + \frac{\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0}{\beta_1} \;-\; \frac{1}{\beta_1}\cdot\tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$ {#eq-ctmax-corrected}

This distinction matters most when asymptotes or curve steepness vary with temperature. If the correction is constant across temperatures, absolute and relative $z$ are the same, although $CT_{max}$ may shift. If the correction changes with temperature, absolute $\log_{10}t_{50}$ is no longer parallel to the midpoint line, and $z_\text{abs}$ can differ from $z_\text{rel}$.

The same logic applies to any fixed mortality threshold. For example, $\log_{10}t_{10}(T)$ is the time to 10% mortality (90% survival) and can identify the onset of population-level mortality, whereas $\log_{10}t_{90}(T)$ is the time to 90% mortality (10% survival) and is closer to population collapse. Any mortality threshold can be estimated from the same posterior by using the corresponding probability in the correction term:

$$
\log_{10} t_m(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\,\log\!\frac{\text{up} - (1-m/100)}{(1-m/100) - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-LTx}

Here, $m$ is the mortality percentage and $1-m/100$ is the corresponding survival probability used in the 4PL. Relative thresholds are useful when differences in asymptotes reflect baseline survival or incomplete response ranges. In contrast, absolute thresholds are useful when the biological question concerns a fixed survival or mortality probability. We therefore recommend reporting which threshold definition was used and, where asymptotes differ strongly among groups or temperatures, checking whether conclusions change under both definitions. In practice, there will often be little difference between relative and absolute summaries (i.e., *z* and $CT_{max}$), but this distinction is important nonetheless.

A powerful aspect of the Bayesian modelling approach described above is that uncertainty in all of the estimated quantities can be derived easily. Additionally, each of the parameters in the model (and therefore *z* and $CT_{max}$) can be estimated separately for different strata (e.g., sex, population, species) in a single joint model by including the relevant fixed covariates and/or random effects in the linear predictor for $\text{mid}$ — and, if appropriate, in the linear predictors for $\text{low}$, $\text{up}$, and $k$ (see @eq-linpred below).  Such an approach allows for *a priori* comparisons (e.g., do sexes or species differ in midpoints?), with correct inference (e.g., do species differ significantly from each other?) and allows for variance decomposition to help identify key sources of variance in the data (e.g., how much variances exists across populations, species, trials etc.?). Such models are most flexibly fit within a Bayesian framework with intuitive interpretation of these parameters and a highly flexible path for error propagation. We show below how such a model can be used to derive all thermal load sensitivity parameters of interest to ecophysiologists. 

# Predicting heat injury and survival under dynamic thermal environments

A particularly powerful aspect of TDT/TLS models are their ability to translate dynamic temperature times series from nature to predict heat injury (HI) accumulation and thus population survival probability [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @li2023; @orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. Heat injury accumulates as the time spent at a given temperature divided by the median lethal time at that temperature, integrated across the temperature trajectory $T(i)$ as follows:

$$
HI(t) \;=\; 100 \int_0^t 10^{\,\big(T(i)\,-\,CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\big)/z} \,di,
$$ {#eq-hi}

where $z$ is the thermal sensitivity and $CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}$ is the static temperature at which 50% of the population reaches the assayed endpoint after one hour of exposure. When $HI(t) = 100\%$, the cumulative dose equals one $t_{50}$ at the 1-hour reference, predicting 50% mortality (or 50% loss of the assayed sublethal endpoint, e.g.\ fertility; @orsted_suzukii_2024). Injury accumulates only when $T(i)$ exceeds a critical temperature $T_{crit}$ below which damage is repaired as fast as it accrues [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_finding_2022]. Field temperature records are typically available at hourly resolution. As such, @eq-hi can be evaluated by summing $100 \cdot 10^{(T_i - CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}})/z}$ across each hourly interval whose temperature $T_i$ exceeds $T_{crit}$, following the implementations in @jorgensen_unifying_2021, @orsted_suzukii_2024, and @li2023. To propagate uncertainty, $z$ and $CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}$, calculated from our Bayesian dose-response model can be used to calculate $HI(t)$ through time by substituting samples from the posterior distribution for each parameter into @eq-hi. 

A more natural prediction to make may instead be the population-level survival probability over time [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. This has the advantage of allowing for a direct connection to population dynamical models which typically use survival, growth and fecundity as vital rates for population-level predictions [@noble2026]. To predict survival, we can calculate the *effective exposure time* at a chosen reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$. More effective time is accumulated at hot temperatures and less at cool ones, in proportion to the scaling factor $10^{(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$,

$$
\tau_{\text{eff}}(t) \;=\; \int_0^t 10^{\,(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,di,
$$ {#eq-tau-eff}

We can then evaluate the 4PL surface once at the cumulative effective exposure to obtain the predicted population-level survival fraction at every point in the trajectory:

$$
S_{\text{pred}}(t) \;=\; S\!\big(T_{\text{ref}},\, \tau_{\text{eff}}(t)\big).
$$ {#eq-S-pred}

Here $S(T, \tau)$ is the survival surface fitted by the dose-response model, so $S_{\text{pred}}(t)$ is the survival probability for an organism held at *constant* $T_{\text{ref}}$ after exposure duration $\tau_{\text{eff}}(t)$. 

# Simulations Evaluating statistical properties of the joint and two-stage modelling approaches

We assessed the performance of the 4PL and two-stage approaches by estimating bias and 95% coverage for *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ across simulation replicates. Bias was calculated as the estimated value minus the true value, whereas coverage was defined as the proportion of intervals containing the true parameter value. The 4PL approach estimated parameter uncertainty directly from the fitted model. In contrast, the two-stage approach required uncertainty to be propagated using the Delta method from the linear model. For the two-stage estimates, we constructed intervals using both normal and *t*-distribution quantiles to evaluate the sensitivity of coverage to different assumptions about sampling uncertainty.

We tested the performance of the two-stage and 4PL approaches across scenarios designed to separate different sources of estimator bias and uncertainty. First, we simulated a strict-equivalence baseline in which survival spanned the full 0–1 range, there was no overdispersion, and only the dose-response midpoint varied with temperature. This scenario tests whether the two approaches recover the same quantities when the assumptions of the classical two-stage model are met. Second, we introduced beta-binomial overdispersion while keeping the same underlying dose-response shape, allowing us to isolate the cost of likelihood misspecification when binomial models are applied to overdispersed count data. Third, we simulated departures from the classical dose-response assumptions by allowing the upper asymptote to decline with temperature, both asymptotes to compress with temperature, or the curve steepness to change with temperature. These scenarios reflect empirical cases in which survival or sublethal responses do not span the full response range, or where the shape of the dose-response curve changes across assay temperatures. Finally, we ran sensitivity analyses that varied the strength of upper-asymptote drift, the overall level of the upper asymptote, and different experimental designs. 

We also evaluated how different experimental designs impact estimates by simulating response curves from several static temperatures and exposure durations with modest numbers of individuals, samples, or replicate vials per treatment [@truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @faber_tdt_plants_2024]. In the simulations, designs included one to five replicate cups per temperature-by-time treatment, with 10 to 20 organisms per cup sampled from a uniform distribution. We also explored experimental designs that conducted experiments over short time spans across the different temperatures which we expected should impact model fit and therefore *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. For each scenario-by-design combination, we simulated 1,000 datasets and fit both the two-stage (both relative and absolute summaries) and 4PL approaches. Overall, this amounted to 29,000 simulations across the 29 scenario-by-design cells. We report Monte Carlo standard errors (MCSE) for the bias and coverage summaries to better understand across simulation uncertainty [@williams2024; @direnzo2023; @koehler2009].

#### *Simulation results*

When the data-generating process matched the classical assumptions, with survival spanning the full 0-1 range and temperature affecting only the dose-response midpoint, the two-stage approach performed similarly to the joint 4PL model, producing little bias and near-nominal coverage when confidence intervals were constructed with *t*-distribution quantiles. This distinction was important: using normal (z) quantiles instead produced anti-conservative intervals with coverage well below the nominal 95% level (@fig-sims & [Supplement 3.1](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#strict-equivalence-baseline) & [3.2](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#likelihood-misspecification-alone)).

In contrast, the two-stage approach resulted in biased *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, often with poor coverage, when temperature impacted upper thresholds (up) and rate of decline (k) (@fig-sims). These biases were consistent even with larger sample sizes (@fig-sims), but were exacerbated with fewer temperature time combinations and low levels of replication (see [Supplement 3.6](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#experimental-design-impacts)). Bias for both *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from the two-stage approaches were particularly severe as the upper survival threshold decreased (@fig-sims2). 

Overall, these results confirm that any model misspecification requires the 4PL to properly recover true *z* and $CT_{max}$, with correct uncertainty. Additionally, with sparse experimental designs or those with short time frames the 4PL model improves estimation of *z* and $CT_{max}$. 

```{r}
#| label: fig-sims
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6.5
#| fig-cap: "Simulation performance when the data-generating process violates classical TDT shape assumptions. Row labelled **'u varies with T'**: upper asymptote $u$ declines 0.01 per °C from 0.92 at $T=34$ °C, with $\\text{low}=0.05$ and $k=8$ constant in $T$. Row labelled **'k varies with T'**: slope $k$ ranges from 6 at 30 °C to 10 at 38 °C, with $u=0.92$ and $\\text{low}=0.05$ constant. Both rows use 5 replicate cups per (T × duration) cell, $N_{sim}=1000$ simulated datasets per row, and beta-binomial overdispersion ($\\phi=5$). Panel (a): mean signed bias in $z$ (left column) and $CT_{max,1h}$ (right column) with 95% Monte Carlo SE bars. Panel (b): empirical 95% interval coverage with the same column layout. Dashed lines mark zero bias and nominal coverage. Absolute-scale methods (`joint_4pl_abs` and all two-stage variants) are evaluated against the analytical absolute-50% truth; `joint_4pl` is evaluated against the relative midpoint truth. Two-stage normal- and t-quantile variants share point estimates and differ only in Stage-2 delta-method interval construction."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + paired colour palette (matches notes writeup) --
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bin_t",
                    "two_stage_bb",  "two_stage_bb_t",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)", "Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)",  "Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)",      "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE) *
                       (1 - mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE)) / dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

# Use n_reps = 5 cells of Scen 3 and Scen 5 for the manuscript figure.
.d <- dplyr::bind_rows(.load_screened("scen3"),
                       .load_screened("scen5")) |>
  dplyr::filter(grepl("_n5$", scenario)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                labels = .method_labels),
    quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                labels = c("z (°C)",
                                           "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
    scenario_label = dplyr::recode(
      sub("_n5$", "", scenario),
      scen3_heat_lowers_u = "u varies with T",
      scen5_sharpen       = "k varies with T"),
    bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    cov_lo  = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,
    cov_hi  = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov
  )

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.p_bias <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate − truth)", y = NULL,
       title = "(a) Bias") +
  .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")

.p_cov <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = coverage, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
  labs(x = "Empirical 95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
       title = "(b) Coverage") +
  .theme_proj

.p_bias / .p_cov + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-sims2
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-cap: "Bias in $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ as the partial-mortality regime is swept along two orthogonal axes. Row labelled **'u drifts with T (varying $\\beta_u$)'**: upper asymptote at $T = \\bar T$ fixed at $u_0 = 0.92$, slope $\\beta_u$ varied across $\\{-0.005, -0.010, -0.015, -0.019\\}$ pp/°C — i.e., $u$ stays high at cool assays and declines progressively at hotter ones. Row labelled **'u is constant but reduced (varying $u_0$)'**: $\\beta_u = 0$ (no temperature drift); the baseline level itself is swept across $\\{0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99\\}$ — i.e., a constant fraction of the cohort is never susceptible, regardless of temperature. Both rows: $\\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, mid linear in $T$ with slope $-0.15$, beta-binomial likelihood ($\\phi=5$), $n_{reps}=5$, $N_{sim}=1000$ datasets per cell. Points are mean signed bias with 95% Monte Carlo SE bars; dashed lines mark zero bias. Left column: $z$; right column: $CT_{max,1h}$. Only the four distinct point-estimate methods are shown: the two-stage Normal-CI and t-CI variants share point estimates within each Stage-1 family, so bias is identical between them; coverage differences appear in the supplementary writeup. Methods are evaluated against the truth on their own threshold scale (see Methods). The two failure signatures differ: drifting $u$ tilts the Stage-2 slope (large $z$ bias, modest $CT_{max,1h}$ bias); constant low $u$ shifts the Stage-2 intercept (large $CT_{max,1h}$ bias, with $z$ bias of opposite sign growing as $u_0$ falls)."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + colour palette ---------------------------------
# Bias is identical for the Normal-CI and t-CI variants of each
# two-stage Stage-1 family (they share point estimates; only Stage-2 CI
# construction differs). For this bias-only figure we therefore plot
# just the four distinct point-estimate methods: two_stage_bin,
# two_stage_bb, joint_4pl (relative), joint_4pl_abs (absolute).
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bb",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin", "Two-stage BB",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)", "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f", "#a63603", "#006d2c", "#a1d99b"),
                         .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

.scen6 <- .load_screened("scen6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = -as.numeric(sub("scen6_ub_m", "", scenario)) / 1000,
                sweep_label = "u drifts with T (varying β_u, pp / °C)")

.scen7 <- .load_screened("scen7") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = as.numeric(sub("scen7_u0_", "", scenario)) / 100,
                sweep_label = "u is constant but reduced (varying u_0)")

.shape_fmt <- function(d) {
  d |>
    dplyr::filter(method %in% .method_levels) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                  labels = .method_labels),
      quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                  labels = c("z (°C)",
                                             "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
      bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias
    )
}

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.build <- function(d, x_label, x_breaks) {
  ggplot(d, aes(x = sweep_x, y = mean_bias, colour = method)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = bias_lo, ymax = bias_hi), width = 0) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    geom_line() +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = x_breaks) +
    labs(x = x_label, y = "Mean bias (estimate − truth)") +
    .theme_proj
}

.p6 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen6),
              x_label  = expression(beta[u]~"(pp / °C, drift of "*italic(u)*" with T)"),
              x_breaks = c(-0.019, -0.015, -0.010, -0.005)) +
  ggtitle("(a) u drifts with T (varying β_u)")

.p7 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen7),
              x_label  = expression(u[0]~"(constant upper asymptote, all T)"),
              x_breaks = c(0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99)) +
  ggtitle("(b) u is constant but reduced (varying u_0)")

.p6 / .p7 + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

# Application of Bayesian dose-response models to case studies

We applied the Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model to four case studies spanning lethal and sublethal thermal tolerance data. Across these examples, we show how the model estimates classical TLS quantities, including $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ and, for lethal endpoints, $T_{crit}$, while estimating and propagating uncertainty to heat-injury and survival predictions. We implemented this workflow in the R package `bayesTLS`; [Supplement 1.1](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-function-tour) provides a package introduction detailing how it can be downloaded and a detailed tutorial using simulated data and the empirical case studies. The `fit_4pl()` function defaults to estimating temperature dependence on all parameters of the 4PL. If no temperature effect exists on a given parameter it will be estimated at zero and it will be equivalent to simpler models. These models also allow for more sophisticated modelling that the two-stage approaches cannot achieve easily. In our supplement, we also show how readers can build greater flexibility into their models. 

The four example case studies we use to validate our modelling approach are: 1) brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), with lethal mortality and sublethal knockdown-time data collected across temperature-duration treatments; 2) snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*) leaf photosystem II performance, measured through chlorophyll-fluorescence-based photosynthetic efficiency; 3) zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) lethal data across early life stages; and 4) vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) mortality and sublethal-response data from @orsted_suzukii_2024. Across these examples, we estimate $z$ and $CT_{max}$ with uncertainty, compare group-specific TLS parameters where appropriate, and use the lethal shrimp and vinegar-fly fits to predict heat injury and survival under field and field-derived temperature traces. There is also interest in estimating $T_{crit}$ in the literature [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019]. $T_{crit}$ can be approximated from lethal data by using the TDT models to estimate the transition point where heat injury begins to accumulate. While there are different methods for estimating $T_{crit}$, our method makes use of existing TDT experimental data.

For the empirical analyses, we fit @eq-4pl in `brms` (v`r as.character(packageVersion("brms"))`; @burkner_brms_2017) with Stan (v`r tryCatch(cmdstanr::cmdstan_version(), error = function(e) "2.38.0")`; @carpenter2017) through `bayesTLS` (v`r as.character(packageVersion("bayesTLS"))`), adding fixed effects and random intercepts to the 4PL parameter predictors where needed. For example, the midpoint can be written as

$$
\text{mid}_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1(T_{ij} - \bar T) + \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\gamma} + b_j,
$$ {#eq-linpred}

where $\mathbf{x}_{ij}$ contains fixed covariates and $b_j$ is a group-level random intercept. Count responses were fit with a beta-binomial likelihood with identity link (brown shrimp, zebrafish, and vinegar fly), while the continuous snow gum PSII proportions were fit with a Beta likelihood with identity link. Random intercepts on $\text{mid}$ accounted for date and tank effects in brown shrimp, experimental date in zebrafish, and day and glasshouse-room effects in snow gum. For group comparisons, `life_stage` in zebrafish and `sex` in vinegar fly were included on $\text{mid}$, with temperature interactions used to estimate group-specific $z$ and $CT_{max}$. To keep the 4PL identifiable, `bayesTLS` constrains $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$ to disjoint intervals and estimates $k$ on a positive scale.

The main case-study models used four MCMC chains with 8000 iterations per chain, including 4000 warmup iterations. The custom joint vinegar-fly sex model used the same Bayesian 4PL structure but a shorter run of 3000 iterations with 1000 warmup iterations. Full formulas, priors, sampler controls, and diagnostics are reported in the [online supplement](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/).

For the heat-injury forecasts, we combined posterior draws from the lethal 4PL fits with `predict_heat_injury()`. For the vinegar fly, we used a real hourly air-temperature series from the hottest-summer station in the @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020 record (Curicó, Los Niches, Chile; January-March 2018). For brown shrimp, we used hourly sea-surface temperatures from the Kristineberg Marine Research Station on the Gullmar fjord, Sweden, a coastal site within the species' range. We used June-August 2018, the warmest summer in the station's 2008-2024 archive. Because this observed record never reached the shrimp's $T_{crit}$ (27.7 °C; 17-year hourly maximum 26.1 °C), we added a +6 °C marine-heatwave scenario that preserved the observed diurnal and seasonal structure but warmed the trace enough to cross the damage threshold. For both species, we predicted heat injury and survival with and without an illustrative damage-repair model.

## Brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*)

Brown shrimp had relatively low thermal sensitivity, high thermal tolerance, and a high $T_{crit}$ for this high-latitude population ([@fig-tls-comparison]A). Under the +6 °C marine-heatwave scenario, heat injury accumulated rapidly once temperatures exceeded $T_{crit}$, leading to strong predicted survival declines with the added repair model delaying this decline but not removing it ([@fig-heat-injury]D-F).

## Snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*)

The snow gum case study demonstrates the same modelling framework for continuous-proportion data, here for photosystem II function measured from chlorophyll fluorescence. Photosystem II function showed the strongest thermal sensitivity among the four case studies ([@fig-tls-comparison]B), illustrating how the 4PL can be used for sublethal physiological endpoints as well as mortality counts.

## Zebrafish (*Danio rerio*)

The zebrafish case study shows how the model can estimate and compare life-stage-specific TLS parameters in a single joint analysis. Larvae were more thermally sensitive than early- and late-stage embryos, giving clear evidence for life-stage differences in $z$ ([@fig-tls-comparison]C). Across the 1-hour reference case studies, estimated $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ ranged from `r ctmax1h_lo` to `r ctmax1h_hi` °C ([@fig-tls-comparison]A-C). 

## Vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*)

The vinegar fly case study shows how the same framework can be used for sex-specific mortality and downstream heat-injury prediction. Our re-analysis of the mortality data from @orsted_suzukii_2024 recovered similar $z$ and $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ estimates for males and females, consistent with their reported values (Table 1: $z$ = 3.28 for males and 3.03 for females; $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ = 35.2 °C for both sexes). We found no sex difference in either quantity, and relative and absolute $z$ and $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ gave similar estimates in this example ([@fig-tls-comparison]D). We also apply our approaches to estimating sublethal sensitivity which we present in the supplement — the [time-to-coma](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-coma) and [reproductive output](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-productivity) analyses — with our values closely matching those of @orsted_suzukii_2024. 

For lethal measures, without repair, cumulative heat injury exceeded one $LT_{50}$ dose and predicted survival declined sharply during the warmest part of the season in Curicó, Chile. Incorporating a repair model, reduced heat injury and offset and predicted mortality, showing how the same fitted 4PL can be combined with alternative assumptions about damage repair ([@fig-heat-injury]A-C).

```{r}
#| label: fig-tls-comparison
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Thermal sensitivity ($z$, left of each panel) and the critical thermal limit ($CT_{max}$, right) estimated by the joint Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model for four case studies: (A) brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), (B) snow gum leaf photosystem II function (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*), (C) zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) across three early life stages, and (D) vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) by sex. Filled ridges are posterior densities, coloured by species and group. The black-outlined point and bar give the posterior median and 95% credible interval, which are also printed beside each distribution. The $CT_{max}$ reference exposure follows each study's own design — 1 hour for brown shrimp, zebrafish, and leaf PSII, and 4 hours for the vinegar fly. Because the grouped case studies (C, D) were fit within a single model, life-stage and sex differences are evaluated as pairwise posterior contrasts, annotated as brackets labelled with $p_{\\mathrm{MCMC}}$ (twice the smaller posterior tail probability of the difference; smaller values indicate stronger evidence that the groups differ)."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig4_case_study_densities.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-heat-injury
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Heat-injury accumulation and predicted survival under natural temperature trajectories for the vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*, panels A–C) and the brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*, panels D–F). (A, D) the temperature trace — real Chilean summer air temperature for the fly (Curicó, Jan–Mar 2018; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020) and real Gullmar fjord summer sea-surface temperature for the shrimp (Kristineberg Marine Research Station, Jun–Aug 2018, the warmest summer on record) warmed by a **+6 °C marine-heatwave projection** — with the relative-threshold $T_{crit}$ (dotted). The fjord itself never reaches the shrimp's $T_{crit}$ (27.7 °C; 17-year maximum 26.1 °C), so the projection makes the heat-injury dynamics visible; it is a scenario, not observed lethal exposure. (B, E) cumulative heat injury (100% = one $LT_{50}$ dose, dashed) and (C, F) predicted survival, each with and without damage repair (colour); fly sexes are distinguished by line type. Bands are ±1 SE across posterior draws. Repair uses an illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield kernel (not empirically fitted)."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig5_heat_injury.png"))
```


# Discussion

We have shown that the Bayesian 4PL dose-response model provides a flexible and statistically rigorous framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity. Simulations show that the model is robust across diverse experimental designs and data conditions, while the case studies recover familiar TLS quantities, with posterior estimates of $z$ falling within the range typically reported for thermal tolerance landscapes (approximately 2-7 °C; @rezende_landscapes_2014). The framework also extends naturally beyond lethal count data to sublethal and non-binomial endpoints, including time-to-event, mixture, and Gamma-distributed responses, while propagating uncertainty into group contrasts and heat-injury predictions. We develop these extensions in the online supplement, including the brown-shrimp [time-to-knockdown](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-shrimp-sublethal) analysis and the vinegar-fly [coma](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-coma) and [productivity](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-productivity) analyses.

The Bayesian model fits the raw response data in a single hierarchical analysis, accommodating diverse experimental designs while partitioning variation among biological and experimental levels. This opens up extensions that are difficult to implement with a two-stage approach. For example, clones, individuals, tissues, populations, or treatments can be included in the same model to estimate genetic, individual, tissue-specific, or environmental sources of variation in $z$ and $CT_{max}$. Full propagation of sampling uncertainty should also make sensitivity and tolerance estimates more useful for robust meta-analyses and comparative studies. Finally, the same framework can be extended to estimate the effects of acclimation, interacting environmental stressors, or other dose-response processes in which biological response depends jointly on stress intensity and exposure duration, including chemical stressors such as herbicides [@arnold_thermal_2025]. More generally, our modelling framework can be extended to any dose-response framework, including toxicology studies where toxicant concentration replaces temperature, opening up new ways to consistently analyse and report sensitivity and tolerance across fields. 

Bayesian methods may be more complicated to implement, however, our `bayesTLS` R package should improve accessibility to experimentalists. Nonetheless, such models can also be fit within a likelihood framework and error propagated using parameteric bootstrapping or the Delta method. An important future development will be to incorporate real rates of repair from experiments [@arnold_thermal_2025; @orsted2026; @buckley_damage_2025] to improve model predictions in nature. By improving how we estimate thermal sensitivity, thermal tolerance, and their biological drivers across populations and species, this framework strengthens our ability to predict the biological impacts of extreme heat in a warming world.

# Acknowledgements

We thank Johannes Overgaard, Joey Bernhardt, Wilco Verbeck, Jennifer Sunday, Ignacio Peralta-Maraver, Arnaud Sentis and Jeff Arnoldi for discussions during an extreme heat workshop funded by an ERC Grant (awarded to Arnaud Sentis: XX), and Gwenaëlle Deconninck, Charlie Cornwallis and Tobias Uller for helpful comments on the ideas developed here. D.W.A.N was supported by an ARC Future Fellowship (FT220100276) and thanks the ANU, Lund University, the ANU Hansen Scandinavian Friendship Grant and the Royal Physiographic Society of Lund for funding that supported the sabbatical during which this paper was written.

# Author contributions {.unnumbered}

D.W.A.N.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. P.P.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. P.A.A.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – Review & Editing. All authors contributed to interpretation of results, reviewed and edited the manuscript, approved the final version, and agree to be accountable for the work.

# Data and code availability {.unnumbered}

All data, analysis scripts, and rendered outputs are available at https://github.com/daniel1noble/bayesTLS. The online supplement, tutorial, and `bayesTLS` installation instructions are available at https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/.

# Conflict of interest {.unnumbered}

The authors declare no competing interests.

# AI Declaration
During the preparation of this manuscript, the authors used generative AI tools, including OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex (vers. 5.5) and Claude Code (vers. opus 4.8), to assist with language editing, structural refinement, mathematical derivations, code building and checking, R package construction and formatting. All scientific ideas, analyses, interpretations, and conclusions were developed and verified by the authors. Any AI-assisted output was critically reviewed and edited by the authors, who accept full responsibility for the final content of the manuscript, supplement, code, and associated R package.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
